**Malabar spinach**, also called **vine spinach**, **Indian spinach**, **climbing spinach**, and **amunututu**,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) refers to the edible plant *Basella alba.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)*[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) It is widely grown in tropical Asia and Africa.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Malabar spinach has dark green, edible leaves that can be ovular or heart-shaped.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) It has a mild, if somewhat peppery flavor.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) When cooked, the leaves become somewhat mucilaginous and taste a good deal like spinach,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) although to a lesser degree than some greens.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The leaves will become more bitter after the plant flowers.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Leaves can be individually trimmed from the plant for use throughout its growth period[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3). It will keep for a couple days but is best when fresh.**[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)**

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Malabar spinach leaves can be eaten either raw or cooked. Young leaves work well in salads,[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) and the slipperiness when cooked works as a thickener in soups.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)
